plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
I will write comments on the tree here:
- MemoryStepCache.cs = feels like bad obp name, and should this belong to the Step/?
- CallStack/RestoredFrame.cs = is it really restoredframe, isn't just a Call, when you snapshot a Call, dont you restore a Call?
- ActionSpec/ExampleSpec = I dont know, feel kind of wrong name, I am not sure what it does. We also had a branch, not to old, this or last weak were we were talking about remove the Catalog (something else owned the info it creates, dont remember), and weren't we also talking here about moving it under Build?
- Callback/Signature.cs = this should not exist, Data contains the Signature
- Channel/Migration.cs = will be removed on next pull
- Serializers = I think the classes can just be Json.cs, instead of JsonStreamSerializer.cs, Plang (what is different between PlangData and Plang), Text (this is Data.Value). 
- SensitivePropertyFilter = just Sensitive, instead of SensitivePropertyFilter, same for Transport?
- Choices = really top level? I feel like it's builder related or something like that
- Config = what is ModuleView.cs??
- Data = PlangTypeConverter.cs, is it not just Converter.cs
- Events/ = I feel like there is just to much sub foldering here, why not just Events/this.cs and Event.cs, I feel like Lifecycle and Bindings are to much
- Providers/ = see comment below about Code
- Services/ = are we using any of this any more? what is it?
- Settings/ = you had some opinion on these classes, I dont see changes in this final tree result.
- ISnapshotted.cs = shouldn't it be ISnapshot?
- Test/ = I see TestFile.cs, TestRun.cs and TestStatus.cs, they dont need prefix Test.

Then I want to talk about the word Provider, I want to change this to Code, and they would implement ICode. This is my thought process. providers is something that and external party can inject into the the language runtime and overwrite it, that is basically code that is being inserted into the runtime, I feel like calling it what it is, what do you think??
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: user
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.anchor: - v3 wanted ReservedKeywords folded into `app.Types/Registry.cs`. ReservedKeywords are *variable names* (`!Test`, `!Signature`, etc.), not types. `app.Variables/Reserved.cs` is the right owner. Tree keeps the Variables placement.

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
Tree revised against your bullets — see the rewritten doc, plus the new "Inline answers to v1 review questions" section that walks through each one in prose.

Applied to the tree (agreed, no further discussion):
- MemoryStepCache → Cache/Memory.cs (rationale: cache subsystem owns the impl variant; not Step's data)
- Callback/Signature/ folder → folded into Callback/this.cs as a property
- Channel/Migration → already gone in the trunk merge (confirmed)
- Serializer Json.cs / Text.cs renames (Stream/Serializer suffixes dropped)
- PropertyFilters/{Sensitive,Transport,View}.cs with parent collection (suffix dropped, missing-collection added)
- Choices → moved to Build/Choices/ (both consumers are build-time)
- Data/Converter.cs (PlangType prefix dropped — namespace is App.Data)
- ISnapshotted → ISnapshot
- Test/{File,Run,Status}.cs (Test prefix dropped — namespace is App.Test)
- Events/ collapsed: Lifecycle layer removed; Before/After become properties on Events/this.cs; Bindings/this.cs and Binding/this.cs sit one level up

Engaged + applied with reasoning:
- RestoredFrame: yes, the name describes a state, not a noun. Tree proposes Call/Position.cs (it identifies *where*). Inline answer explains why a separate type is still needed (lifecycle differs from live Call).

Still open (flagged in Open judgment calls):
- Catalog under Build/: I don't have memory of the prior thread — please point me at it. Lean if pressed: full move to Build/Catalog/, with TypeEntry relocating to App/Types.
- PlangSerializer vs PlangDataSerializer: depends on whether application/plang is dead transport. I'll trace usage when stage 1 lands.
- Provider → Code: real design question, not auto-applying. Inline section walks the tradeoff. Short version: "Code" is too generic to be a type-system identifier; the rename only carries weight if it's a story-level concept change ("runtime no longer talks about Providers"). What's the underlying concept change?

Answered as information (no tree change):
- ModuleView.cs: per-module typed settings view returned by app.Config.For<T>(context); generic; runtime-used
- Services: in active use — per-outbound-call I/O scope (HTTP/TCP/WS); referenced from App.this.cs:229
- Settings: I had no specific prior opinion; the tree is OBP-clean as-is (SqliteSettingsStore is the per-impl variant, same shape as Cache/Memory.cs)

OpenAi static-counter todo logged in Documentation/Runtime2/todos.md.

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
PropertyFilters = maybe just Filters, does it need to define Property, I dont think it does.
TimeSpanIso8601Converter.cs and TypeJsonConverter.cs smells , should we make Converters folder and name them better, TimeSpanIso8601 and Json? Not sure about UnregisteredMimeType.cs you tell me what your opinion is??
DefaultGrepProvider.cs = should we maybe just call it Grep (it is already in Providers folder, and no real point of having Default there??)

What is your opinion on ISettingsStore.cs, SettingsVariable.cs & SqliteSettingsStore.cs, is it not just ISettings.cs, Variable.cs and Sqlite.cs? maybe it should have a Provider folder where Sqlite.cs is located?

OpenAiProvider.cs = OpenAi.cs, not need for provider in the name, it located in a provider folder
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: user

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
Five answers. Recurring shape worth naming at the end.

**1. PropertyFilters → Filters** — agree. `App/Filters/{Sensitive,Transport,View}.cs` with `Filters/this.cs` as the collection. Interface becomes `IFilter` (namespace `App.Filters` disambiguates). "Filters" is broad enough that if a non-property filter concept appears later (event filtering, e.g.), it co-locates without renaming.

**2. TimeSpanIso8601Converter / TypeJsonConverter / UnregisteredMimeType** — agree on the smell, but a top-level `App/Converters/` folder is the same mistake as `App/Json/`: "Converter" is mechanism, not domain. Lean per file:

- **TimeSpanIso8601Converter** → flat rename to `App/Channels/Serializers/TimeSpanIso8601.cs`. One file, no subfolder. The Serializers folder owns serialization mechanism; the file lives there.
- **TypeJsonConverter** → relocates to `App/Data/Json.cs` (lives with the `Type` it serves). Wrinkle: this collides with the prior-settled rename `PlangTypeConverter → App/Data/Converter.cs`. Both are converters for `App.Data.Type` via different mechanisms (`System.ComponentModel.TypeConverter` for Newtonsoft, `JsonConverter<Type>` for System.Text.Json). Lean: split by mechanism — `App/Data/Converter.cs` (component-model) and `App/Data/Json.cs` (System.Text.Json). Different consumers, different files.
- **UnregisteredMimeType** — its a typed exception (`: System.Exception`) thrown by the serializer registry. Exception names ARE compound by convention (System.IO.FileNotFoundException, KeyNotFoundException) — thats not Rule A. Lean: keep the name. If a second sibling exception ever appears in `Serializers/`, fold them into an `Errors/` subfolder then.

**3. DefaultGrepProvider → Grep** — agree, drop both `Default` and `Provider`. `App/Data/Providers/Grep.cs` clean. Interface `IGrepProvider` similarly drops to `IGrep`. (Independent of the still-open Provider→Code rename — works either way.)

**4. Settings shape** — partial agree, walking it:
- `ISettingsStore` → `IStore` (not `ISettings`). The interface defines persistence operations; namespace `App.Settings` already says Settings. `IStore` is the contract.
- `SettingsVariable` → `Variable.cs`. Namespace `App.Settings.Variable` disambiguates from `App.Variables.Variable` (different things — one intercepts `%Settings.X%`, the other is the variable system).
- `SqliteSettingsStore` → `Sqlite.cs`. On the Provider/ subfolder: lean **no** for now, matching the prior-settled `Cache/Memory.cs` convention (one impl, flat file). The subfolder anticipates a second impl that doesnt exist. If/when an in-memory or other store lands, refactor to `Stores/{Sqlite,Memory}.cs` at that point.

**5. OpenAiProvider → OpenAi** — agree. `App/modules/llm/providers/OpenAi.cs`. Folder says provider; file just names which one.

---

**Recurring shape across all five**: dont repeat the folder in the file name. PropertyFilter (folder = Filters), TimeSpanIso8601*Converter* (folder = Serializers, role implicit), DefaultGrep*Provider* (folder = Providers), Sqlite*SettingsStore* (folder = Settings), OpenAi*Provider* (folder = providers). Same anti-pattern five times. This is essentially Rule A (compound name) but specifically for the case where the suffix duplicates the parent folder. Worth promoting to its own bullet under Rule A or carving as Rule F if you want it called out separately. Lean: bullet under Rule A — "if the role suffix names the parent folder, drop the suffix." Want me to draft that addendum?
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
Conceded — ISettings, not IStore. My mistake.

I had assumed Settings already had a @this above the store (Settings.@this using an internal Store abstraction), which would make IStore the right backend-role name. Checked — there is no Settings/this.cs. The store IS the public API: `actor.SettingsStore.Get(...)` is how settings are accessed, no wrapper above it.

With that, IStore is just a generic role word and ISettings is the contract name. Matches .NET idiom (IConfiguration not IConfig; IServiceProvider not IProvider) — the interface uses the namespace + concept; implementations name the variant.

Final:
```
App/Settings/
├── ISettings.cs       (was ISettingsStore)
├── Variable.cs        (was SettingsVariable)
└── Sqlite.cs          (was SqliteSettingsStore)
```
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
Yes — that reframe is right, and its sharper than what I had.

Clarification on the rewrap: `SettingsVariable` does NOT wrap `App.Variables.Variable`. Different concepts:
- `App.Variables.Variable` is a `record` that names a variable — used as `Data<Variable>` for action parameters that take write targets.
- `SettingsVariable` is a `Data.@this` subclass with a custom `GetChild` that intercepts `%Settings.ApiKey%` and lazy-loads from the store.

No wrapping happening. But the bigger point lands hard: SettingsVariable IS the navigable Settings entity. Mounted on every actors Variables under the name "Settings". Dot-navigation goes through it to lazy-load from the store. Thats the role of a this.cs. The name `SettingsVariable` was the smell — named after implementation mechanism (a Data object participating in variable resolution) rather than its role (the Settings root).

Final shape:
```
App/Settings/
├── this.cs        (was SettingsVariable; navigable entity, intercepts %Settings.X%)
├── IStore.cs      (was ISettingsStore; persistence interface used by @this)
└── Sqlite.cs      (was SqliteSettingsStore; SQLite impl of IStore)
```

And this resurrects IStore (which I conceded too fast last round). Once Settings has a real @this, the interface IS a backend abstraction — the @this uses a Store internally — and IStore names that role correctly. ISettings would now be wrong because it would compete with this.cs for the role of "the Settings contract."
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
Right — I was carrying the inheritance smell forward. Re-walking:

The current `SettingsVariable` wears two hats:
1. **Runtime mode** (with `_app`) — mounted on Variables under "Settings", intercepts dot-navigation, calls the store
2. **Storage mode** (JsonConstructor) — represents a single loaded value, no navigation

Two roles in one class, and the `: Data.@this` inheritance exists only to fit through the variable-resolution interception path. Mechanism leaking into shape — exactly the inheritance-over-composition pattern youre pulling away from.

Your shape:

```
App/Settings/
├── this.cs        Collection — Set/Get on Data values (like Goals/this.cs)
├── IStore.cs      Persistence interface
└── Sqlite.cs      SQLite impl, JSON-serializes Data via PlangSerializer
```

Settings.@this is a collection holding Data values keyed by name. Sqlite (implements IStore) handles Data→JSON for persistence. Data itself is the value type — no subclassing, its already what we want to store. The JsonConstructor/Runtime dual-mode disappears because the two responsibilities split: @this is the navigation+API root, store is the load/save layer.

**One thing to settle when this carves as a stage**: how does `%Settings.ApiKey%` reach `settings.Get("ApiKey")` at variable-resolution time? Today it works because SettingsVariable IS-A Data on the Variables map, so the GetChild dispatch finds it. With the rewrite, settings.@this isnt a Data subclass — the variable system needs a different mechanism to recognize "Settings" as a navigable mount and dispatch into it.

Two shapes:
- **A.** Variables grows a registration for non-Data navigable mounts: `variables.RegisterNavigable("Settings", path => settings.Get(path))`. The resolver consults this map.
- **B.** A small interface (`INavigable` or similar) that the variable system checks for during compound-name resolution.

Lean **A** — keeps the variable-resolution concern OUT of Settings interface. Settings just exposes Get/Set; the variable system is the consumer that wires itself in. But this wants settling when the stage carves, not during tree planning.

Flagging this as Open Judgment Call on the tree. Want it carved as its own dedicated stage, or folded into an existing one?
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text: Settled — option A: `variables.RegisterNavigable(name, resolver)`. Beyond Settings, this gives a clean extension point for any future non-Data navigable mount, which is the better reason than mine. Will carve as a Settings stage and capture the RegisterNavigable mechanism as part of it. (Also unblocks similar cleanups if other navigable mounts surface.)
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
All four applied to tree, plus Rule A sub-rule added.

**Tree changes:**
- `PropertyFilters/` → `Filters/`; files renamed (`Sensitive.cs`, `Transport.cs`, `View.cs`, `this.cs`)
- `TimeSpanIso8601Converter.cs` → `TimeSpanIso8601.cs` (flat in Serializers/)
- `TypeJsonConverter.cs` → relocates to `App/Data/Json.cs` (lives with Type)
- `UnregisteredMimeType.cs` kept (typed exception)
- `DefaultGrepProvider.cs` → `Providers/Grep.cs`; `IGrepProvider.cs` → `IGrep.cs`
- `OpenAiProvider.cs` → `OpenAi.cs`

**Prose updated:**
- Old `SensitivePropertyFilter` section rewritten to reflect the Filters/ folder rename.
- Old `PlangTypeConverter.cs — agreed` section expanded into a full "Converters cluster" section covering all three files plus the App/Converters/ rejection (same logic as App/Json/).
- Two new short sections: `UnregisteredMimeType` (kept), `DefaultGrepProvider/OpenAiProvider` (agreed).
- Rename summary at the bottom updated (now 25 files renamed).

**Principles update — Rule A gains a sub-rule:**
```
Sub-rule: role suffix duplicates the parent folder. If the class names role-pattern suffix names the folder it lives in, drop the suffix — the folder already says it. SensitivePropertyFilter.cs in PropertyFilters/ becomes Sensitive.cs in Filters/. DefaultGrepProvider.cs in Providers/ becomes Grep.cs. OpenAiProvider.cs in providers/ becomes OpenAi.cs. SqliteSettingsStore.cs in Settings/ becomes Sqlite.cs. The fully-qualified type read as App.{Folder}.{File} reads naturally without the redundant decoration. Same logic for Default* prefixes when theres only one impl variant in the folder.
```

Also noted typed exceptions stay compound (FileNotFoundException, UnregisteredMimeType) — thats the carve-out so the screen doesnt false-positive on `: System.Exception` types.
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text: isn't it C, it's about how we (de)serialize stuff? unrelated to this folder structure, I feel like we are getting to many case differences errors, could that be?
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: user

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
Yes, Rule C — JSON options are static fields with no @this owner, so the cleanup applies. The interesting part is your case-mismatch concern: it's a real risk if options disperse across consumers (two consumers, two opinions on case sensitivity → silent serialization mismatch).

Updated tree: lean shifts from v1's "(a) disperse" to **(b) single home — App/Json/this.cs** with options as instance properties (app.Json.SnapshotClone, app.Json.PrWrite, etc). Rule C is preserved (the data has an owner, even if it's a config-shaped owner) and there's one source of truth for case/property conventions. Codified in Open judgment call A in the doc.
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text: I dont like  App/Json   Json is a serialzation format it doesn't deserve to be root object, did I misunderstand this maybe?
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: user

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
Right — you're correct. Json is a serialization format, not a domain entity. App/Json/ as a root mount was wrong; reverting that decision.

Updated tree: Utils/Json.cs is deleted; each consumer owns its own JsonSerializerOptions instance. Snapshot already inlines its options for clone semantics; Build/PrWrite holds its own; Data.Envelope already inlines _envelopeJsonOptions; each Serializer/{Json,Plang/this,Plang/Data,Text}.cs holds its own internals.

My earlier 'case-mismatch' worry was overblown — the options ARE mostly consumer-specific in their actual content; the 'shared' feel was a junk-drawer artifact in Utils/Json.cs. When two consumers genuinely need identical options, that's a design conversation at that point, not a justification for a synthetic root home.

Open question A resolved: disperse to consumers.
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text: or Catalog/Spec/Example.cs and Action.cs?
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: user
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.anchor: │   • llm/providers/OpenAiProvider.cs        (private static int _requestCount → DELETED, stage 15; temp blocker confirmed by Ingi 2026-05-07; todo logged)

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
Yes — folded into the Modules.Schema shape. With Catalog dissolving (your runtime-uses-builder correction settled this), the records relocate as:

```
App/Modules/Schema/
├── Spec/
│   ├── Action.cs              (← Catalog/ActionSpec.cs)
│   └── Example.cs             (← Catalog/ExampleSpec.cs)
├── Entry.cs                   (← Catalog/TypeEntry.cs)
├── ExampleHelpers.cs          (kept for action-author ergonomics)
├── Render.cs                  (← Catalog/ExampleRenderer.cs; instance, navigates this.Modules)
└── this.cs                    (← Catalog/this.cs; instance Build(), no parameter)
```

Flat files inside Spec/ (matches the existing record-as-flat-file convention — CallStack/Diff.cs, CallStack/Flags.cs, Errors/Error.cs). If you want the full OBP nested form (Spec/Action/this.cs and Spec/Example/this.cs), happy to push there — just costlier in folder count for record types.
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.anchor: │   • llm/providers/OpenAiProvider.cs        (private static int _requestCount → DELETED, stage 15; temp blocker confirmed by Ingi 2026-05-07; todo logged)

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text: ExampleHelpers = is this right location? is it right name?
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: user
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.anchor: │   • llm/providers/OpenAiProvider.cs        (private static int _requestCount → DELETED, stage 15; temp blocker confirmed by Ingi 2026-05-07; todo logged)

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
Both wrong, and the cleanest fix is delete it.

**The name** — 'Helpers' is Rule A red flag (role suffix on a noun). **The location** — it's not its own concept; it just wraps the ExampleSpec record constructor.

Looking at the existing code: ExampleHelpers has one static method `Example(intent, params chain)` that just calls `new ExampleSpec(intent, chain)`. C# records have positional constructors that work with `new` directly — the helper is leftover from pre-record-syntax convenience.

Fix: delete ExampleHelpers entirely. Authors switch from `Example("intent", chain)` to `new Example("intent", chain)`. One-line migration per ExamplesForLlm() site. Tree updated.
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.anchor: │   • llm/providers/OpenAiProvider.cs        (private static int _requestCount → DELETED, stage 15; temp blocker confirmed by Ingi 2026-05-07; todo logged)

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text: should all be readonly, I kind like the app/variables/reserved.cs location
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: user

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text: Settled: Variables/Reserved.cs as the home, all const/readonly. The current mutable `public static string Test = "!Test"` and the `static List<string> keywords = new()` get fixed alongside the move (stage 15).
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text: not sure what it's used for so cant answer
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: user

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
Looked at the use sites. Fires once per child App after construction (OsDirectory inherited, Testing.IsEnabled set, CurrentTest assigned), before the test's entry goal runs. Six probes in PLang.Tests/App/Testing/RunActionTests.cs subscribe to assert child-App state (parallel count, OsDirectory, IsEnabled).

It's static because there's no other way for the test to reach the child App before its first goal runs — children are constructed inside the test runner's parallel loop and aren't returned to the caller. The static event is the only hook point.

Architect's lean: the test runner (App.Test.@this) should own a list of child-app callbacks. Static-ness moves onto the test runner, which is itself ~singleton per test process. Detail in Open judgment call G in the doc. Leaving this comment open until stage 15 carves and the rewrite shape settles.
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
I think we need to improve this commenting tool, this text "not sure what it's used for so cant answer" is pointing to line 273 which doesn't seem to exist anymore so bit lost, maybe we should store the line text with the comment so we can at least know what it was about. what do you say?? this is the python webserver fix.

regarding the code that comment applies, I think we can just leave it for now, unless you feel it's important to fix
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: user

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
Tool issue noted — line-anchored comments drift when the doc changes. Two practical workarounds inside the current tool:

1. **Quote a snippet of the line text** in the comment body (the original L273 was about App/Json/ + ReservedKeywords; with the App/Json/ revert, that area shifted).
2. **Section-anchor** instead of line — comment on a heading line (the section name doesn't move as much as content lines).

Real fix is on the tool side: store an anchor token (line content hash or section path) and re-locate the comment after edits. Not architect work but flagging here. As for the original ChildAppCreated comment (id 000d10d61a) — still open in stage 15's design queue; lean is test-runner-owned registry, settled when stage 15 carves.
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text: if it's same code, then shouldn't it be removed? or is it not same code?
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: user

plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.text:
Different filters, not duplicate code. Sensitive strips secrets; Transport strips runtime-only properties for serialization; View filters by the [Default/Store/LlmBuilder/Debug/Out/In] view tag. Same shape (interface), different rules.

Rename agreed: drop the PropertyFilter suffix → Sensitive.cs, Transport.cs, View.cs (your earlier bullet). By Rule B, three siblings with the same shape want a parent collection, so the tree adds PropertyFilters/this.cs as the registry. Folded into stage 14.
plan/post-cleanup-tree.md.author: architect

plan/principles.md.text: plang p build does not exist, where did you find that?? plus I want the cli to just use "plang --builder", no "plang build"
plan/principles.md.author: user

plan/principles.md.text:
Right — `plang p build` was fictional, and the CLI form should align with everything else as a flag, not a verb. Fixed in two places:

- principles.md Rule D table: collapsed the two CLI rows into one — `plang build` (today, wrong) → `plang --builder` (after, correct). Dropped the verb-commands-stay-verbs carve-out paragraph; it had no concrete anchor in the codebase since the CLI is flag-driven.
- plan.md stage 16: removed the `(commands plang p build / plang p test stay verbs)` parenthetical; CLI line now reads `plang build → plang --builder, plang --test → plang --tester`.

Verified against Executor.cs:34 — today accepts `plang build` and `plang --build` as the same form; test side is already `--test`. Rule D itself stands; only the bogus illustration was removed.
plan/principles.md.author: architect

plan/scope-map.md.text: and when we create new threads, such as '- call goal Run, dont wait' - this is async call where we dont wait for the answer and a new context is created, belonging to the same actor ofc... hmmm.... and actually I suppose system context as well... hmm... well see when we get there, this isn't implemented yet, just wanted to note it.
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: - **Per-context** — one instance per Actor.Context. Today this is essentially "per-actor" because each Actor owns one Context, but the distinction matters for things scoped to an execution context (Trace).
plan/scope-map.md.author: user

plan/scope-map.md.text: Noted. The per-context vocabulary already accommodates this — when async-call-without-wait lands, the spawned work gets its own Context belonging to the same actor. Added to the doc under "Acknowledged for the future, not actionable now." Will update if the construct ships before this cleanup completes.
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: - **Per-context** — one instance per Actor.Context. Today this is essentially "per-actor" because each Actor owns one Context, but the distinction matters for things scoped to an execution context (Trace).
plan/scope-map.md.author: architect

plan/scope-map.md.text:
I reading this doc, and I am bit confusing myself. So for console, sure all good. For web request, then I suppose we have app pool, rent it and return it on each request. the app object
  would be reset to rented state on return. what confuses me is thing like app.Debug

I want to have in my code

'- set debug = true' which basically whould set debug mode on my context and no other, same goes for builder, '- build my.goal', or testing, '- run tests on '/tests'', 
'- add before '/admin' call Authenitcate' <= event created on web request

when you say app.Errors , app.Snapshot, app.Callback, app.Events on instance, so if user has error and system has error, are they in same thing. that feels wrong, app.callback feels like per actor, 

So as you can see I am a bit confused, and the mental model is clearly not clear. 

I see you have comments below, you can maybe answer these, dig into the code also, dont assume. then maybe update the Mixed cases

General:
app.Navigators not sure why this is here, this is per Data, but I see it exists on app as property, I would have thought this is more of a Variables thing since Data is stored and retrieved in the Variables.
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: | Property | Type | Reason for shared scope |
plan/scope-map.md.author: user

plan/scope-map.md.text:
Dug into the code; your intuition was right on the substantive items. Findings:

**Settled by digging:**

- **Events** — drift, not just shared. `Context.Events` exists per-Context (Actor/Context/this.cs:92) AND `App.Events` exists. ALL real registrations go through `Context.Events` (event/on.cs:65, mock/action.cs:73, test/run.cs:120). The App-level one is unused dead drift. Future stage: delete `app.Events`, unify on Context.Events. Events is *already* per-actor in practice.

- **Errors** — Trail is run-wide (App-shared) but `_current` is AsyncLocal (flow-scoped). User and System errors mix into the same Trail. Your intuition is right — **Trail should be per-actor**. The AsyncLocal-current stays correct.

- **Snapshot** — `app.Snapshot` doesnt actually exist as a property. `Snapshot.@this` is instantiated per-operation (App.this.Snapshot.cs:18, CallStack/this.Snapshot.cs:94, Callback/ErrorCallback.cs:102 — each `new Snapshot.@this()`). My map was wrong. Removed from shared; added a new "Per-operation" section.

- **Callback** — confirmed shared is correct. Its purely *config* (`app.Callback.Signature.ExpiresInMs`), not a callback registry. Actual callbacks live with the Data they belong to. App-level config makes sense.

**Architectural direction noted but bigger than this plan:**

- **Build/Debug/Testing as per-context mode flags** — your `- set debug = true`, `- build my.goal`, `- run tests on /tests`, `- add before /admin call Authenticate` examples describe per-context modes. Today these are app-level CLI flags (`plang --builder`, `--debug`, `--tester` set them at launch). The shift to per-context is a real architectural direction but bigger than this cleanup plan — added a section "App-level today, but the scope is wrong or contested" naming it as a future direction. Probably its own plan after this one lands.

**Navigators (general comment)** — today on App, registered at boot via `Navigators.RegisterDefaults()`. Your point that its per-Data and feels like a Variables thing is real — added as Open Question 7. Worth deciding whether `Navigators` moves under `Variables/` or `Data/`, or stays on App.

Updated the scope-map: corrected the shared list (removed Snapshot, Catalog, Choices), added two new sections (App-level-but-scope-wrong, Per-operation), added Context.Events to the per-actor list, rewrote the Open questions to separate "settled by digging" from "still genuinely open."

Read through the updated doc and let me know which of the still-open questions (1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 8) want decisions now vs deferred to follow-up plans.
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: | Property | Type | Reason for shared scope |
plan/scope-map.md.author: architect

plan/scope-map.md.text:
you can move app.build, .debug, .testing up to app level, we wont change this in this branch
not sure about rest, Events seems huge task, or maybe we just need to chat about it
.Trail I have no idea, what do you say?
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: These properties exist on `App.@this` today but the actual usage pattern doesn't match "one shared instance per app." Each is flagged for what's true now and what Ingi's intuition says it should be.
plan/scope-map.md.author: user

plan/scope-map.md.text: Done. Build/Debug/Testing moved back to the shared (App-level) list with notes that per-context mode flags are a future direction not addressed in this branch. Events kept in a slimmer drift section flagged "deferred for chat." For Errors.Trail — my read: shared isnt actively breaking; "all errors that happened in this app run" is a coherent meaning for a diagnostic Trail, and no current consumer needs per-actor isolation. Lean: leave shared, no change in this branch. Revisit if a per-actor use case (e.g., per-actor error reporting in a UI) ever surfaces. Captured that as the recommendation in the doc.
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: | `app.Navigators` | `Data.Navigators.@this` | Data navigation registry. ★ Open question 7 — Ingi questions whether this belongs on App or under Variables. |

plan/scope-map.md.text: It is one per app.
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: After stage 13 (`settings-collection-rework`): `Settings/this.cs` becomes a collection over Data; the SettingsVariable absorption changes the shape. The new question is: is the resulting `Settings.@this` shared (one per app) or per-actor? Per the per-actor `SettingsStore`, it's likely *per-actor* — because the storage is per-actor. **Need to settle.**
plan/scope-map.md.author: user

plan/scope-map.md.text: there is only going to be system.sqlite for the settings, on per app, using system.sqlite
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: **2. `actor.SettingsStore` — per-actor SQLite, but shared storage paths?**
plan/scope-map.md.author: user

plan/scope-map.md.text: Settled — Settings.@this is shared (one per app). Updated Mixed cases section + stage 13 description in plan.md to reflect this.
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: Today: one `SettingsVariable` lives on App; it gets registered on every actor's `Context.Variables` (Actor.this.cs:133). Navigation hook exists once per actor; the object itself is shared.
plan/scope-map.md.author: architect

plan/scope-map.md.text: Settled — SettingsStore moves from per-actor (todays drift) to App level (`app.SettingsStore` backed by `system.sqlite`). Verified: every real consumer already goes through `app.System.SettingsStore` (Goals/Setup, identity provider, llm provider, settings module); zero callers ever use Users store. The per-actor `_dataSource` field on Actor is dead drift. Folded into stage 13s scope. Updated plan.md stage 13 description: collection-over-Data + app-level SettingsStore relocation + RegisterNavigable mechanism.
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: After stage 13 (`settings-collection-rework`): the `Settings.@this` collection is **shared (one per app)** — Ingi confirmed 2026-05-08. There's only going to be `system.sqlite` for settings (per-app, not per-actor). The current per-actor allocation in `Actor.@this._dataSource` is dead drift; future stage 13 work also moves SettingsStore to App level.
plan/scope-map.md.author: architect

plan/scope-map.md.text: what is the question?
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: After stage 7 (`callstack-promote-app-property`), the property moves from `app.Debug.CallStack` to `app.CallStack` directly. Still shared.
plan/scope-map.md.author: user

plan/scope-map.md.text: Youre right, there wasnt one — that was a stray sentence ending without a question. Reworded the CallStack section to drop the implied question and just state the shared-data, multiple-paths-to-reach-it pattern (App owns the tree; Context exposes a read-through view; stage 7 promotes the property).
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: The CallStack tree is one per app run. `Context.CallStack` is a read-through accessor (`Context/this.cs:48: CallStack => App.Debug?.CallStack`). Data lives shared on App; navigation path also exposed through Context. Not a scope mismatch — conventional shared-data, multiple-paths-to-reach-it pattern. Stage 7 promotes the property to `app.CallStack` directly.
plan/scope-map.md.author: architect

plan/scope-map.md.text: "The CallStack tree is one per app run." is it tho? isn't it per context? isn't App.Debug.CallStack just config? make sure, because if callstack is on App.Debug I think we are in trouble
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: The CallStack tree is one per app run. `Context.CallStack` is a read-through accessor (`Context/this.cs:48: CallStack => App.Debug?.CallStack`). Data lives shared on App; navigation path also exposed through Context. Not a scope mismatch — conventional shared-data, multiple-paths-to-reach-it pattern. Stage 7 promotes the property to `app.CallStack` directly.
plan/scope-map.md.author: user

plan/scope-map.md.text:
Youre right to worry. Dug in — `App/Debug/this.cs:101` allocates one CallStack per app; inside it, `_current` is correctly AsyncLocal (flow-scoped), but `_root`, `Audit`, and the tree structure are shared. Under parallel execution (web-pool, concurrent Contexts), pushes from different request flows interleave into one tree; Audit mixes traces; `%!callStack%` may show frames from a concurrent flow. Sequential CLI works; parallel doesnt.

Kept as-is for this branch per your call — stage 7 stays a pure property-promotion (`app.Debug.CallStack` → `app.CallStack`); scope remains shared. Filed in `Documentation/Runtime2/todos.md` 2026-05-08 with the full analysis (per-context vs split-config-from-state options) for when this becomes the active concern. Updated scope-map.md Mixed-cases entry to reflect "shared today, scope wrong for parallel, kept as-is, todo filed."
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: Future stage (stage 13): move to `app.SettingsStore` (one shared instance backed by `system.sqlite`); drop the per-actor `_dataSource` field; sweep `app.System.SettingsStore` references to `app.SettingsStore`.
plan/scope-map.md.author: architect

plan/scope-map.md.text: you mean we should remove the shortcuts?
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: These two app-root properties are conveniences that hide the per-actor reality. Call sites using `app.Variables` are implicitly per-actor (the *current* actor). That's the same anti-pattern flagged in `principles.md` ("implicit per-actor dependencies dressed up as app-level reaches"). Worth a future cleanup pass — likely fold into stage 10 (`app-run-redesign`) when App.Run gets clarified.
plan/scope-map.md.author: user

plan/scope-map.md.text: it actually migth be issue having these shortcuts, if we run paralell, we have the same app but new context, so maybe we should remove it
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: These two app-root properties are conveniences that hide the per-actor reality. Call sites using `app.Variables` are implicitly per-actor (the *current* actor). That's the same anti-pattern flagged in `principles.md` ("implicit per-actor dependencies dressed up as app-level reaches"). Worth a future cleanup pass — likely fold into stage 10 (`app-run-redesign`) when App.Run gets clarified.
plan/scope-map.md.author: user

plan/scope-map.md.text: Yes, settled both (yours below confirmed it). The shortcuts get removed — theyre fragile under parallel execution (same App, multiple Contexts in flight, the shortcut returns whichever actor is current per AsyncLocal which is non-deterministic in the parallel case). Updated Mixed cases section to mark as settled-remove. Folded into stage 10s scope (`app-run-redesign` already touches Apps surface; the shortcut sweep fits there). Stage 10 description in plan.md updated. If the sweep turns out too big to fold, it carves out as its own small stage.
plan/scope-map.md.author: architect

plan/scope-map.md.text: whichever, you choose
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: 3. **`Channel.@this.App` back-ref after stage 1** — once `Channel.@this.Channels` exists, App is reachable via `Channels.Actor.App`. Remove in a future stage, or keep both?
plan/scope-map.md.author: user

plan/scope-map.md.text: Choose: **remove** in a future stage. Once stage 1s `Channel.Channels` back-ref lands, App is reachable via `Channels.Actor.App`; the direct `Channel.App` back-ref is redundant and violates the single-navigation-point OBP discipline. Filed as **stage 20** (`channel-app-backref-drop`) — small mechanical sweep depending on stage 1.
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: - **Snapshot** is per-operation, not shared. Map corrected.

plan/scope-map.md.text: per app. this is global cache

plan/scope-map.md.text: Settled — shared per app. Updated annotation in shared list to make the design intent explicit.
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: - **Events de facto pattern** — PLang `event.on` writes to `Context.Events` (per-actor), the middle of the three intentional tiers. (Earlier this map called `app.Events` "dead drift" — that was wrong; correction recorded above.)
plan/scope-map.md.author: architect

plan/scope-map.md.text: I think it should move yes
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: 7. **`app.Navigators` placement** — Ingi's general comment: "this is per Data… I would have thought this is more of a Variables thing since Data is stored and retrieved in the Variables." Today registered at boot via `Navigators.RegisterDefaults()` on App. Move under Variables/ or Data/, or keep on App?
plan/scope-map.md.author: user

plan/scope-map.md.text: Confirmed move. Filed as **stage 21** (`navigators-to-variables`): folder `App/Data/Navigators/` → `App/Variables/Navigators/`; namespace `App.Data.Navigators` → `App.Variables.Navigators`; property `app.Navigators` → `app.Variables.Navigators`; boot registration `Navigators.RegisterDefaults()` moves to where Variables is allocated. Pure folder relocation + namespace rename + caller sweep; no behaviour change. Tree updated to show Navigators under Variables/.
plan/scope-map.md.anchor: - **Errors `_current`** is correctly AsyncLocal (flow-scoped); only Trail was at issue (now settled above).
